section.title: Blessed Are Those Who Fear the Lord
section.sectionRationale: A Song of Ascents, ninth in the pilgrim collection Psalms 120 to 134, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. Following Psalm 127's confession that the LORD alone builds the house, this psalm pictures the ordinary, flourishing life of the household built and blessed by Him.
section.thesis: Blessing rests on everyone who fears the LORD and walks in His ways, seen in fruitful labor, a flourishing family, and the wider peace of Zion, a blessing that flows from and is fulfilled in Christ, who perfectly feared the LORD and secures every spiritual blessing for His people.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the blessing of fruitful labor for the one who fears the LORD, verses 1 to 2, the blessing of a flourishing family, verses 3 to 4, and the closing blessing of peace from Zion for generations to come, verses 5 to 6.

units.label: Blessed are all who fear the LORD

verses.text: Blessed are all who {{yare|fear}} the LORD, who walk in His ways!
For when you eat the fruit of your labor, blessings and prosperity will be yours.

units.label: A fruitful vine and olive shoots

verses.text: Your wife will be like a fruitful vine flourishing within your house, your sons like olive shoots sitting around your table.
In this way indeed shall blessing come to the man who {{yare|fears}} the LORD.
May the LORD bless you from Zion, that you may see the prosperity of Jerusalem all the days of your life,
that you may see your children's children. Peace be upon Israel!

yare.gloss: To fear, revere, stand in reverential awe; not terror but a whole-life submission to the LORD that shapes obedient walking in His ways, verses 1 and 4.

basic.context: This Song of Ascents pictures the ordinary blessings that flow from reverent obedience to the LORD, fruitful labor, a flourishing household, and generations to come, all set within the wider peace and prosperity of Zion.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes the blessed pattern of an Israelite household under the covenant, and it prescribes as an abiding truth that reverence for the LORD, walking in His ways, is the foundation of a genuinely flourishing life, whatever its particular circumstances.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm opens and closes its central section with yare, fear of the LORD, reverent awe that shapes obedient living, the same reverence Proverbs 1:7 calls the beginning of wisdom.
basic.typology: The fruitful vine within the house anticipates Christ, the true vine in whom His people abide and bear fruit, and the fear of the LORD perfectly embodied in this psalm's ideal man anticipates Christ, on whom the Spirit of the fear of the LORD rests without measure.
basic.christ: Isaiah 11:2-3 describes the coming Messiah as delighting in the fear of the LORD, the perfect fulfillment of the blessed man this psalm describes, and Jesus calls His people to abide in Him as the true vine, John 15:4-5, so that their lives bear the fruit this psalm celebrates.
basic.god: The LORD blesses reverent obedience with fruitful labor, a flourishing household, and the sight of generations to come, and He is the source of Zion's peace and prosperity that overflows into the life of every household that fears Him.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 11:2-3 pictures the Messiah delighting in the fear of the LORD. John 15:4-5 calls believers to abide in Christ, the true vine, to bear fruit. Ephesians 1:3 declares that God has blessed believers with every spiritual blessing in Christ.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's picture of blessing flowing from the fear of the LORD is fulfilled in Christ, who perfectly feared and obeyed the Father, and in whom, as the true vine, His people are grafted to bear lasting fruit and receive every spiritual blessing.

context.literary: A Song of Ascents structured around a chiastic frame of blessing, verse 1 and verse 4, surrounding the concrete picture of fruitful labor and a flourishing family, before widening out to the blessing of Zion and future generations.
context.historical: Likely paired in use with Psalm 127, since both address household life under the LORD's blessing, the one confessing that the LORD builds the house, this one describing what that blessed household looks like in daily life.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 127's confession that the LORD alone builds the house with a vivid picture of what that LORD-built household actually looks like, and anticipates the New Testament's picture of every spiritual blessing given in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach that genuine blessing, fruitful labor, a flourishing family, and generations to come, flows from reverent fear of the LORD and obedient walking in His ways, not from human effort alone.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes the ordinary shape of a blessed Israelite household under the covenant. It prescribes as an abiding truth that fearing the LORD and walking in His ways is the foundation of a flourishing life, while not promising identical material outcomes to every believer in every circumstance.

debates.question: Does this psalm promise material prosperity and family flourishing to every believer who fears the LORD?

views.label: A covenantal pattern with real, though not absolute, expectation
views.case: The psalm reflects the ordinary blessing God attaches to covenant faithfulness under the old covenant, and such blessings often, though not invariably, still accompany godly living.

views.label: A picture of ultimate blessing, not a guarantee in every case
views.case: Scripture elsewhere shows faithful believers who suffer loss, barrenness, or hardship, so this psalm describes the shape of covenant blessing in general rather than promising it without exception to every individual.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm teaches that true flourishing is found in reverent obedience to the LORD, while pastoral wisdom must temper any expectation that every godly household will experience identical material blessing.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm is framed by yare, the fear of the LORD, in verses 1 and 4, the reverent awe Proverbs 1:7 calls the beginning of wisdom, shaping a household whose fruitfulness is pictured in the vine and olive shoots of verse 3.

typology.type: The fruitful vine within the house, verse 3
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true vine, in whom believers abide and bear lasting fruit, John 15:4-5.

typology.type: The man who fears the LORD, verses 1 and 4
typology.fulfillment: Christ, on whom the Spirit of the fear of the LORD rests without measure, Isaiah 11:2-3.

study.christ: Isaiah 11:2-3 pictures the coming Messiah as one who delights in the fear of the LORD, the perfect and final fulfillment of the blessed man this psalm describes. Jesus calls His people to abide in Him as the true vine, John 15:4-5, so that the fruitfulness this psalm pictures in an earthly household becomes, in Christ, the abundant spiritual fruit of a life joined to Him. In Him, believers receive every spiritual blessing, Ephesians 1:3, the deeper and lasting reality behind this psalm's picture of blessing.
study.god: The LORD blesses reverent obedience with fruitful labor, a flourishing household, and the sight of generations to come, and He is the source of Zion's peace and prosperity that overflows from His presence into the life of every household that fears Him.

crossReferences.note: Pictures the Messiah delighting in the fear of the LORD, the perfect fulfillment of this psalm's blessed man.
Calls believers to abide in Christ, the true vine, so that they bear lasting fruit, as this psalm's vine flourishes within the house.
Declares every spiritual blessing given to believers in Christ, the fuller reality behind this psalm's picture of blessing.

study.oneStory: This psalm's picture of blessing flowing from the fear of the LORD is fulfilled in Christ, who perfectly feared and obeyed the Father, and in whom, as the true vine, His people are grafted to bear lasting fruit and receive every spiritual blessing, both now and in the peace of the Zion still to come.

sermon.title: Count Your Blessings
sermon.why: Begg treats Psalms 126 to 128 together, showing how this psalm's picture of a flourishing household under the fear of the LORD completes the sequence's teaching on God's blessing of ordinary life.

quiz.q: According to verse 1, who is called blessed?

options.text: All who fear the LORD and walk in His ways.
options.note: This is the exact condition of blessing the psalm names at the outset.

options.text: Only those born into wealthy families.
options.note: The psalm ties blessing to reverence and obedience, not birth or wealth.

options.text: Anyone who works hard, regardless of their relationship to God.
options.note: The psalm specifically ties blessing to fearing the LORD, not mere hard work alone.

options.text: Only the priests serving in the temple.
options.note: The blessing described is for any person who fears the LORD, not a priestly class alone.

quiz.q: What does verse 2 say results from eating the fruit of your labor?

options.text: Blessings and prosperity will be yours.
options.note: This is the exact promise attached to fruitful labor for the one who fears the LORD.

options.text: Endless anxiety about the future.
options.note: The psalm promises blessing and prosperity, the opposite of anxiety.

options.text: A guarantee of never working again.
options.note: The verse assumes ongoing labor whose fruit is blessed, not the cessation of work.

options.text: Immediate exemption from all hardship.
options.note: The psalm promises blessing on labor, not a blanket exemption from every hardship.

quiz.q: How does verse 3 picture the wife and children of the one who fears the LORD?

options.text: A fruitful vine within the house and olive shoots around the table.
options.note: These are the exact images the psalm uses for a flourishing family.

options.text: A barren field and empty table.
options.note: The psalm's images are of fruitfulness and abundance, the opposite of barrenness.

options.text: A distant relationship with little contact.
options.note: The image is intimate and flourishing, gathered around the table, not distant.

options.text: A source of constant conflict.
options.note: The psalm's picture is one of flourishing and joy, not conflict.

quiz.q: What does the psalm pray for in verses 5 to 6 regarding Zion and generations?

options.text: That the LORD would bless from Zion and grant sight of children's children, with peace upon Israel.
options.note: This is the exact content of the closing prayer and blessing.

options.text: That Zion's prosperity would end within the reader's lifetime.
options.note: The prayer is for prosperity to continue all the days of one's life, not to end.

options.text: That the reader would leave Jerusalem permanently.
options.note: The prayer is for seeing Jerusalem's ongoing prosperity, not for leaving it.

options.text: That only the reader's generation would be blessed, with no future generations in view.
options.note: The prayer explicitly includes seeing one's children's children, future generations.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament deepen this psalm's picture of a fruitful vine?

options.text: Jesus calls believers to abide in Him as the true vine so that they bear lasting fruit.
options.note: John 15:4-5 takes up the vine imagery and applies it to union with Christ.

options.text: It rejects vine imagery as belonging only to the old covenant.
options.note: Jesus explicitly uses this very vine imagery of Himself and His disciples.

options.text: It applies the vine only to literal agriculture, never to spiritual life.
options.note: John 15 applies the vine directly to spiritual union with Christ, not mere agriculture.

options.text: It teaches that fruitfulness now depends entirely on human effort alone.
options.note: John 15 teaches that fruit comes from abiding in Christ, not from unaided human effort.

questions: The psalm ties blessing to fearing the LORD and walking in His ways. What would it look like this week to let reverence for God shape an ordinary decision you face?

The psalm pictures a flourishing household as a fruit of God's blessing. Whether or not your own family looks like this picture, how do you see God's fruitfulness at work in your life and relationships?
The prayer widens from personal blessing to the peace of the whole people of God. Who beyond your own household are you praying for God to bless and keep in peace?
Jesus calls His people to abide in Him as the true vine in order to bear fruit. Where do you need to depend more on Christ rather than your own effort to produce lasting fruit in your life?
The psalm ends by looking toward future generations, children's children. What are you doing now to plant faith and blessing that will outlast your own lifetime?

translationNotes.note: Fear of the LORD, yare, denotes reverential awe leading to obedience, the same disposition Proverbs 1:7 calls the beginning of wisdom, not mere terror.
Olive shoots around the table pictures young olive saplings growing beside a mature tree, an image of vigorous new life gathered close to its source.
Bless you from Zion locates the source of personal blessing in the LORD's chosen dwelling place, tying individual flourishing to the wider life and worship of God's people.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 128 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 124-132). Verse count (6) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 128:1-6. No em or en dashes present in the source text. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's sermon 'Count Your Blessings' (covering Psalms 126-128) at truthforlife.org.
